planting.note: Direct sow or transplant after last frost in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile, moist loamy soil in full sun; sponge gourd is a vigorous tropical vine requiring a sturdy trellis or fence, at least 10 feet of vertical space, and a long, hot growing season — at least 150 frost-free days for the fruit to develop and the fiber to mature. Start indoors 3-4 weeks before transplanting in short-season areas.

directions: Direct sow or transplant after last frost in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile, moist loamy soil in full sun; sponge gourd is best known as the source of the loofah sponge — the fibrous interior skeleton of the mature fruit that becomes the natural bath and scrubbing sponge found in health food stores worldwide. The fruits are edible when young and immature (before the fibrous network fully develops) — harvested at 4–6 inches long and cooked like zucchini or opo squash, with a mild, slightly mucilagenous texture popular in East Asian and South Asian cuisine (called 'ghi tow' in Chinese, 'louki' or 'turai' in Hindi). To harvest loofahs, allow the fruit to mature and dry completely on the vine until the outer skin turns brown and papery and rattles when shaken — then peel the dry skin, shake out the seeds (save some for replanting), and rinse the fibrous sponge. A highly productive vine requiring a long, hot summer; grow in a greenhouse or polytunnel in short-season climates. The ornamental yellow flowers attract pollinators.

planting.note: Direct sow or transplant after last frost in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile to average soil in full sun; balsam apple is closely related to but distinct from bitter melon (Momordica charantia). It requires a trellis and warm, humid conditions. Native to tropical Africa and naturalized across many tropical regions.

directions: Direct sow or transplant after last frost in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile to average soil in full sun; balsam apple is the African species of the bitter gourd group — a climbing vine with deeply lobed, attractive leaves and small, oval, prominently warty orange-yellow fruits (smaller and rounder than bitter melon) that split dramatically when ripe to reveal bright scarlet-coated seeds in a striking display. Native to tropical and subtropical Africa and widely naturalized across Africa, South Asia, and northern Australia. The leaves, young shoots, and unripe fruits are eaten as cooked vegetables across Africa, where it is an important pot herb and relish ingredient in many regional cuisines. The ripe fruit is ornamental and was traditionally used medicinally as a topical treatment for skin conditions (the name 'balsam' refers to the resinous, aromatic properties historically attributed to the fruit). More compact and less bitter than bitter melon (M. charantia). The red-seeded, splitting fruit display is striking and ornamental in the garden.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 8-10 weeks before last frost; transplant after soil warms to well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile, moist loamy soil in full sun. Gboma eggplant is a vigorous grower in warm, humid conditions and produces large plants requiring staking. More heat and humidity tolerant than standard eggplant cultivars.

directions: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before transplanting after last frost in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile, moist loamy soil in full sun; gboma eggplant is one of the most important vegetable plants of tropical West and Central Africa — a large, robust eggplant species (distinct from the common eggplant Solanum melongena) with large, lobed, hairy leaves and rounded to oblong, whitish-green to purple-streaked fruits. The leaves, young shoots, and immature fruits are all cooked and eaten as vegetables in West African cuisine — the leaves are particularly important as a cooked vegetable eaten with fufu (pounded starchy staples) and soups across Nigeria, Ghana, Cameroon, and neighboring countries. An important traditional African food plant adapted to hot, humid tropical conditions where standard eggplant varieties may struggle. The fruits can also be used like conventional eggplant in curries and stews. More robust and heat-tolerant than standard eggplant cultivars. A valuable addition to home gardens in zone 9–12 where traditional West African vegetables are of interest.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to lean, loamy or sandy soil in full sun; Amaranthus hybridus is a parent species of cultivated grain amaranths and an important leaf vegetable in many parts of Africa, Asia, and the Caribbean. It self-sows prolifically and can become weedy.

directions: Direct sow after last frost in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to lean, loamy or sandy soil in full sun; hybrid amaranth (also called slim amaranth, green amaranth, or quelite in Mexican Spanish) is one of the most important wild and cultivated leaf vegetables in the tropical and subtropical world — a coarse, weedy-to-cultivated annual of the amaranth family widely consumed as a cooked green leaf vegetable (called 'alefu' in Ethiopia, 'callaloo' in the Caribbean, 'rajgira' leaves in India) across Africa, South Asia, and the Caribbean. The tender young leaves and shoot tips are harvested and cooked like spinach — mild in flavor, nutritious, and fast-growing in warm weather. One of the parent species of the cultivated grain amaranths (Amaranthus cruentus and others) selected for large seed production. Self-sows prolifically and establishes easily in hot, dry, disturbed soil — one of the most adaptable and heat-tolerant leaf vegetables available. Harvest young leaves continuously through the summer; the plant bolts in heat to produce seed heads used as grain in traditional cultures.

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring or fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile, moist loamy soil in full sun to partial shade; mizuna is a cool-season crop that tolerates light frost (to about 25°F) and bolts in warm temperatures above 75°F. In zone 8-10 (Chester's area), grow in fall through spring; provide shade cloth in early spring to delay bolting.

directions: Direct sow in early spring or fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile, moist loamy soil in full sun to partial shade; mizuna is a Japanese mustard green with deeply lobed, finely cut, feathery leaves and a mild, pleasantly peppery flavor — more delicate and less bitter than many other Asian mustard greens. One of the most versatile and popular salad greens in Japanese cuisine (used raw in salads, as a garnish, and in hot pot dishes) and increasingly popular in Western salad mixes, where its attractive, deeply serrated leaf shape adds visual interest. Extremely cold-hardy and fast-growing — one of the best cool-season salad greens for extending the harvest season in spring and fall. In zone 8–10 Gulf Coast gardens (Chester's area), it is ideal as a fall through winter through early spring crop, planted in September for fall harvest through March. Tolerates partial shade better than many salad crops, making it suitable for interplanting under taller crops. 'Baby' leaves are harvested with scissors for salad mixes; full plants are harvested at 6–8 weeks.
